questions.question: What is the difference between map(), filter(), and reduce()?
questions.answer: map(fn): Applies fn to each element and returns a NEW array of the same length. filter(fn): Returns a NEW array containing only elements where fn returns true. reduce(fn, initial): Applies fn(accumulator, currentValue) to fold the array into a single value — the most powerful but hardest to read. All three are non-mutating (pure). Together they form functional data pipelines: filter → map → reduce.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of common array operations?
questions.answer: push/pop (end): O(1). unshift/shift (front): O(n) — all elements must shift. access by index: O(1). indexOf/find: O(n) — linear scan. slice: O(k) where k = slice length. sort: O(n log n). splice (insert/remove middle): O(n). For frequent front insertions, use a deque (Python collections.deque) or linked list.

questions.question: How does JavaScript spread (...) differ from Object.assign for arrays?
questions.answer: Spread creates a shallow copy: [...arr] creates a new array with the same element references. Object.assign({}, obj) is similar for objects. Spread is now preferred for arrays and objects. Both are shallow — nested objects/arrays are still shared. For deep copying, use JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) (limited to JSON-serializable types) or the structuredClone() API (modern browsers/Node 17+).

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency List Comprehensions queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
